An immediate concern in interpreting these results could be that the job changes on which we base our measure of human capital transferability are simply job changes within diversified firms in the sample, inducing reverse causality. However, recall that we use only job changes in which workers exit their original employing firm to construct our indexes, ruling out this possibility. Moreover, we consider only job changes in which some, but not all, of the workers from a firm change jobs in a given quarter. This restriction prevents changes in firm identifiers due to mergers from contaminating our sample of job changes. Thus, our tests indeed isolate a tendency for diversified firms to operate in sets of industries between which worker skills easily transfer. Moreover, the existence of the association between this transferability and firm composition in the cross-section—and its relative strength compared to product market links—suggests that human capital transferability is a first-order determinant of firm scope.
article.7 Conclusion:
We study the transferability of human capital as a motivation for diversifying acquisitions. We use worker-firm matched data from 31 states available from the LEHD program to construct a measure of this transferability using the frequency of worker job changes between industries. We find that human capital transferability not only predicts the intensity with which firms diversify into other industries but also predicts the intensity more strongly than measures of product market relatedness. Moreover, it predicts the industry composition of diversified firms in the cross-section.

We also find that firms reap tangible benefits from diversifying into high HCT industries with respect to their existing divisions. Firms that acquire industries with greater human capital transferability with their existing industries enjoy larger increases in labor productivity and are less likely to divest their newly acquired industries subsequently.

Finally, we find evidence consistent with the human capital channel at the worker level. Acquiring firms that diversify into industries with high human capital transferability with their existing operations are more likely to retain skilled workers following the deal and to transfer workers from the target firm to other industries in the merged firm.

Our results challenge the view that diversification across unrelated product markets destroys value or is a manifestation of agency problems. Instead, we identify potential synergies from diversification based on the transferability of human capital across industries and the benefits of internal labor markets. Moreover, our theory does not require that acquirer, target, or merged firms are operating inefficiently to justify the transactions. Thus, it is likely to be a mistake to equate all diversification with a failure of governance. Though we do not claim that all diversifying deals create value or that there is no role for agency models, our results provide one explanation for why many large, successful firms in U.S. markets are diversified. An interesting avenue for additional study would be to link general patterns in labor mobility with trends in corporate restructuring activity over longer time horizons.

Our results also have implications for how we think about firm groupings, like industries, in finance and economics. Traditionally, researchers have focused exclusively on product market considerations to group firms. An industry consists of firms that sell similar products. Similarly, industries are related if the products produced by one industry are used as inputs for the other. We instead identify human capital as an important common factor across firms. Recent asset pricing research argues that this commonality is a risk factor in equity markets (Eisfeldt and Papanikolaou 2013;Donangelo 2014). We argue that human capital transferability is also an important determinant of firm boundaries. Another opportunity for future research is to build on this evidence, considering the implications of human capital shocks for corporate outcomes in much the same way traditional corporate finance considers product market industry shocks.

25 Strict enforcement of noncompetes also reduces the (within-industry) outside options of a firm’s workers, which could make them more willing to accept an internal redeployment.

26 In industry pairs with very low HCT (e.g., agriculture and microchips), the costs from redeploying workers to jobs for which their skill sets are a poor match are likely to dominate adjustment cost savings, even where NCA enforcement is strict. Thus, these pairs will be irrelevant to our test, which conditions on the existence of a diversifying deal.

27 We provide a more detailed development of the theory behind our empirical specification in the Internet Appendix .

28 The seven dimensions capture (1) whether the state has a statute governing enforcement, (2) how broadly an employer’s protected interest is defined, (3) how strong the employer’s burden of proof is to establish existence of an enforceable NCA, (4) whether signing an NCA at employment inception suffices for enforceability, (5) whether continued employment suffices for enforceability of an NCA entered after the employment relationship has begun, (6) whether courts can modify overbroad NCAs, and (7) whether the NCA is enforceable if the employee is fired.
